content:
✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: I like bit manipulation challenges. This one is simple.  By the way,  the reference solution could be even shorter using popcount() instead of a loop of shifts ;-)

Nice test indeed.  Fast and short should be the active.  
I'd have expected an edge case close to 29 bits of 10000000 to ensure that the solution encoded can handle it.  But your existing case of 2015501 would de facto already require a 17 bit number so de facto the use of a 32 bit one (I don't think many languages offer integers between 16 and 32). Moreover, there is no risk to be bothered by the sign bit.  So for me it's ok !
Nice code test, that involves thinking to make short. Maybe expand to also detect when there are above and/or below a certain number of 1's to get around the binary output issue.
this is perfect for quick mode in clash of code

❌ Rejected the contribution.
Comment: * Binary output issue
* No formatting of variables and constants
* Inadequate test coverage

Binary output issue
* According to the contribution guidelines, binary outputs should be avoided for clashes. That's exactly the issue with your clash, as it allows only two possible answers (either "YES" or "NO"). Please modify the output requirement to allow for more than two possible answers.

Formatting
* Please study the formatting syntax on this page: https://www.codingame.com/playgrounds/40701/help-center/statement , and format your statement properly as described in the points below.

Goal
* Please format "YES" and "NO" as {{constants}}.

Input
* Please format "num" as a [[variable]].

Output
* Please change "the input number" to "[[num]]".

* Please format "YES" and "NO" as {{constants}}.

Constraints
* Please format "num" as a [[variable]].

* Please refer to @N3l's comment and fix the constraint and/or the cases.

Cases
* Please refer to @N3l's comment and add new pairs of cases.

* Please note that each test and its validator should cover the same scenario with slightly different inputs. For this reason, the default case names are organised in pairs (Test 1 / Validator 1, Test 2 / Validator 2, etc.). There is no need to renumber them intentionally, and your cases should follow the same pairing structure.

Thanks.

Nice for shortest mode. 
But there is a mismatch between your stated constraints (max 1000) and your actual test cases (which exceed 2,000,000) :)

I also suggest adding edge cases like powers of 2 (e.g., 1024) to ensure solutions are robust.

Please note that binary output should be avoided for clashes according to the contribution guidelines (see Specific Guidelines point 4):

https://www.codingame.com/playgrounds/40701/help-center/contribution-guidelines

Please kindly reject the contribution instead of approving it until the issue is fixed. Thanks.
According to Gemini it means something friendly like "you are on my mind".
